name: GERD (Heartburn)
ontario_ma_scope: ⚠️ GERD/heartburn is NOT in the Ontario MA prescribing program. Pharmacist role: OTC counselling for mild/intermittent symptoms, Rx adaptation for existing therapy, referral for new/alarm symptoms.

key_questions: Typical symptoms: retrosternal burning, regurgitation, worsened post-prandially/lying down?
Alarm symptoms (dysphagia, weight loss, GI bleeding, vomiting)?
Age ≥60 with new-onset symptoms (increased risk of Barrett's/cancer — ACG 2022 threshold)?
Current medications worsening GERD (CCBs, nitrates, TCAs, bisphosphonates)?
Heaviness of Smoking Index (HSI) if applicable; long-term PPI use — reassess indication?
red_flags: Dysphagia or odynophagia → structural pathology — endoscopy
Unintentional weight loss + GI symptoms → malignancy
GI bleeding (hematemesis, melena) → upper GI bleed — ER
Age ≥60 with new-onset symptoms → endoscopy to exclude Barrett's oesophagus/cancer (ACG 2022; note CAG uses different threshold — reassess with specialist)
Iron deficiency anaemia without clear cause → upper GI workup
Persistent vomiting → structural or motility pathology

first_line.name: Proton Pump Inhibitor
first_line.drugs: Omeprazole (Losec / Olex OTC 20 mg) 20 mg OD 30–60 min before breakfast × 8 weeks | Pantoprazole (Pantoloc) 20–40 mg OD before breakfast
first_line.notes: Most effective acid suppression. Take 30–60 min BEFORE breakfast — proton pumps must be active for PPIs to work. Standard initial symptomatic trial: 8 weeks (ACG 2022), then reassess for step-down/deprescribing. For on-demand GERD (appropriate for non-erosive reflux disease/NERD only — NOT suitable for erosive esophagitis, which requires endoscopic diagnosis beyond pharmacist MA scope): omeprazole 20 mg when symptoms occur. Long-term: annual review of indication; step-down if possible. OTC omeprazole 20 mg (Olex, Losec OTC) available in Canada without Rx — advise max 14 days OTC use then reassess. DRUG INTERACTIONS: Omeprazole/esomeprazole significantly inhibit CYP2C19 → reduce clopidogrel efficacy (use pantoprazole or rabeprazole instead in patients on clopidogrel). PPIs reduce absorption of ketoconazole, itraconazole, atazanavir, levothyroxine, oral iron (separate by 2h), dasatinib. DEPRESCRIBING: For long-term PPI users without ongoing indication — consider stepwise deprescribing (step down to H2RA or PRN) to avoid rebound acid hypersecretion. Wean over 2–4 weeks. Note: ranitidine (Zantac) permanently withdrawn from Canadian market in 2020 due to NDMA contamination — use famotidine or PPI instead.
first_line.preferred_when: Moderate-frequent heartburn (>2×/week); erosive esophagitis (endoscopy-confirmed — refer for diagnosis)

second_line.name: H2 Receptor Antagonist
second_line.notes: Faster onset than PPIs (30 min vs 1–4h) — useful for breakthrough or PRN. Less effective than PPI for severe or frequent GERD. Tachyphylaxis develops with regular use. Can use famotidine at bedtime + morning PPI for nocturnal breakthrough. Note: ranitidine (Zantac) permanently withdrawn from Canadian market in 2020 due to NDMA contamination — use famotidine instead.

second_line.name: Antacids
second_line.drugs: Calcium carbonate (Tums) | Aluminum/magnesium hydroxide (Gaviscon, Maalox) PRN
second_line.notes: Immediate relief (minutes) but short duration (30–60 min). PRN for mild breakthrough. Gaviscon: forms raft over stomach contents — useful for regurgitation. Not for regular scheduled use. In pregnancy: lifestyle + antacids (calcium carbonate) first-line; ranitidine withdrawn — use famotidine if H2RA needed; PPIs (omeprazole, pantoprazole) acceptable if symptoms persist.

non_pharm: Head of bed elevation 15–20 cm (for nocturnal symptoms — most effective lifestyle intervention)
Lose weight if overweight (most evidence-based lifestyle intervention)
Avoid trigger foods (individualized): coffee, citrus, tomatoes, spicy foods, high-fat meals, chocolate, mint
Small frequent meals rather than large meals
refer_when: New onset in patient ≥60 years (alarm feature — endoscopy to exclude Barrett's/malignancy; ACG 2022 threshold; CAG uses different threshold — reassess with specialist)
GI bleeding (melena or hematemesis) → urgent ER referral
Iron deficiency anaemia (alarm feature → urgent referral)
Failure to respond to PPI × 8 weeks
Suspected atypical/extra-esophageal GERD (chronic cough, hoarseness, asthma)
Recurrent GERD requiring ongoing PPI — reassess indication, consider GI referral

references: Ontario College of Pharmacists. Minor Ailments: Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease (GERD). OCP Prescribing Standards, O. Reg. 256/24 (2025).

Armstrong D, et al. Canadian Consensus Conference on the Management of Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease in Adults — Update 2004. Can J Gastroenterol. 2005;19(1):15–35. (supplemented by RxTx 2023 updates).
Katz PO, et al. ACG Clinical Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease. Am J Gastroenterol. 2022;117(1):27–56.
NICE. Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease and dyspepsia in adults: investigation and management. NICE guideline [CG184]. National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (2014, updated 2023).
